Strategy.text: How is Sustainability reflected in your overall corporate strategy?
Strategy.option_a: I am not aware that Sustainability is part of our corporate strategy
Strategy.option_b: There is a rather short mention of Sustainability in our strategy
Strategy.option_c: We do consider Sustainability in our strategy and we are working to reflect it better
Strategy.option_d: Sustainability is one of the supporting elements in our strategy and there is some detail behind it
Strategy.option_e: Sustainability is a core element of our strategy and critical for the existence and growth of our business

Strategy.text: How is Sustainability reflected in the values of your organisation?
Strategy.option_b: We care but do not explicitly consider it as a differentiating value
Strategy.option_c: We are planning to make Sustainability one of our corporate values
Strategy.option_d: Sustainability is one of our values
Strategy.option_e: Sustainability is one of our core values and we monitor how it is exhibited in our culture on a regular basis

Strategy.text: Is there board-level oversight of climate-related issues within your organisation and how does this operate?
Strategy.option_b: There is limited oversight and we consider climate-related issues in our board discussions from time to time
Strategy.option_c: Our board actively discusses (and tackles) climate-related issues
Strategy.option_d: Yes, they review climate-related issues at board level on a regular basis
Strategy.option_e: Yes, there is a board member who has responsibility for progressing climate-related issues and we have a solid agenda for it

Strategy.text: Does your organisation have a process for identifying, assessing, and responding to climate-related risks and opportunities?
Strategy.option_b: We are working on developing a structured and standard process
Strategy.option_c: Yes, we have a basic process in place that needs improvement
Strategy.option_d: Yes, we have a process in place and we follow it on as needed basis
Strategy.option_e: Yes, we have a mature and detailed process in place that is regularly reviewed and updated

Strategy.text: Do you benchmark your Sustainability progress against your industry peers?
Strategy.option_b: We are currently considering performing a benchmarking study
Strategy.option_c: Yes, we have done it in the past, but it is not a scheduled event
Strategy.option_d: Yes, we do it, but it is done on an ad hoc basis when requested by our CEO or the board
Strategy.option_e: Yes, we conduct a benchmarking exercise at least every 6-12 months

Strategy.text: How does your organisation approach external climate disclosure and reporting frameworks?
Strategy.option_a: We do not report externally on climate or sustainability matters
Strategy.option_b: We are aware of frameworks such as CDP, TCFD, and CSRD but have not yet made formal submissions or adopted structured reporting
Strategy.option_c: We report against one or more frameworks on a voluntary basis, though coverage and data quality are still maturing
Strategy.option_d: We report against established frameworks (such as CDP, TCFD, or CSRD) with reasonable data quality and consistent methodology, reviewed annually
Strategy.option_e: Our external climate disclosure is independently assured, covers all material scope categories, and is aligned with leading frameworks; we actively use the process to drive internal improvement

Strategy.text: Do you have a science-based net-zero (or similar) target for your organisation?
Strategy.option_b: We are working on establishing science-based targets
Strategy.option_c: We are in the process of developing a Sustainability target, which is not quite net-zero yet
Strategy.option_d: Yes, we have net-zero target, but it's not science-based
Strategy.option_e: Yes, we have a science-based net-zero target that has been reviewed by an external party

Strategy.text: Do you have a defined roadmap to reach your net-zero target?
Strategy.option_b: We are creating a net-zero roadmap this year
Strategy.option_c: Yes, we have a high level net-zero roadmap that is being reviewed and resourced
Strategy.option_d: Yes, we have a detailed roadmap to reach our net-zero target that is sufficiently defined to be able to see the steps to reach net-zero

Strategy.text: How do you manage execution of your net-zero roadmap?
Strategy.option_b: We are in the process of agreeing the roadmap and its execution approach
Strategy.option_c: Our Sustainability manager is responsible for execution of the roadmap
Strategy.option_d: We have a small Sustainability team that is responsible for execution of our roadmap
Strategy.option_e: We have a Sustainability team in place supported by established programme management principles to execute our net-zero roadmap

Strategy.text: How are your net-zero targets and roadmap milestones reflected throughout your organisation?
Strategy.option_a: They are not or we don't have the targets
Strategy.option_b: We are working on translating our net-zero target into targets for all our departments
Strategy.option_c: Most of our leadership team have some net-zero related targets
Strategy.option_d: All leadership team and most of our managers have some net-zero related targets
Strategy.option_e: Each of our managers and departments across the whole organisation have a relevant portion of the overall net-zero target in their agenda to deliver

Strategy.text: How is environmental impact reflected in your IT/digital services strategy?
Strategy.option_b: We are currently considering how to reflect environmental impact in our IT/digital services strategy
Strategy.option_c: Our environmental impact is mentioned at a high level in our IT/digital services strategy
Strategy.option_d: Our IT/digital services strategy includes a specific section about our environmental impact and it is one of the core elements of our strategy

Strategy.text: Do you have a net-zero (or similar) target for your IT & digital services?
Strategy.option_b: We are working on defining a target for climate-related issues
Strategy.option_c: We have a climate-related target for the business, but not for IT services
Strategy.option_d: We have a target for our IT/digital services
Strategy.option_e: Yes, we have a science-based target for IT/digital services

Strategy.text: Are your IT sustainability targets expressed as efficiency or intensity metrics — consumption per unit of output — rather than solely as absolute reduction targets?
Strategy.option_a: Our sustainability targets, where they exist, are expressed only as absolute figures — total emissions, total energy — with no reference to efficiency or intensity
Strategy.option_b: We understand the distinction between absolute and intensity targets but have not yet adopted intensity metrics; our targets remain absolute
Strategy.option_c: Some intensity metrics are used informally alongside absolute targets in certain domains (e.g. PUE in data centres, cost per workload in cloud), but they are not embedded in formal targets or governance
Strategy.option_d: Intensity metrics are formally defined and tracked alongside absolute targets for major IT domains; leadership receives both views and uses them in performance conversations
Strategy.option_e: Intensity metrics are the primary lens for sustainability performance across all IT domains — consumption per transaction, per user, per unit of business output — with absolute targets as a secondary view; intensity improvement targets are embedded in leadership accountability frameworks and reviewed at board level

Strategy.text: How is Sustainability reflected in your IT governance structure?
Strategy.option_a: Our IT governance structure does not include Sustainability
Strategy.option_b: We are working on making Sustainability a more explicit part of our IT governance
Strategy.option_c: We have a Sustainability representative in our organisation
Strategy.option_d: We are reviewing our IT governance structure to include Sustainability in it
Strategy.option_e: Sustainability issues have a firm and clear place in our IT governance structure

Strategy.text: Who is responsible for executing the Sustainability strategy of your IT/digital services?
Strategy.option_a: We don't have a Sustainability strategy covering IT/digital services
Strategy.option_b: Our leadership team has the responsibility, but they have no dedicated team
Strategy.option_c: We are creating & resourcing a team to ensure our Sustainability strategy is well executed, this team will report to senior leaders
Strategy.option_d: We have a dedicated team, but they don't have aligned budget as yet
Strategy.option_e: We have a dedicated team with a defined budget. They are responsible for execution, timelines, budgets, KPIs and targets

Strategy.text: How do you monitor the execution of your Sustainability roadmap in IT/digital services?
Strategy.option_b: We publish roadmap updates from time to time
Strategy.option_c: We have a set of metrics to track execution of our Sustainability roadmap
Strategy.option_d: We have a person who gathers metrics once per year/six months and prepares a report for the leadership team
Strategy.option_e: We have a Sustainability programme office that monitors execution and reports on all metrics every quarter

Strategy.text: Are there Sustainability targets included in the performance/bonus contracts of the senior IT leadership team?
Strategy.option_a: No, there are no specific targets
Strategy.option_b: Yes, I think some of the leadership team have targets included in their bonus plans
Strategy.option_c: Yes, our IT director has a Sustainability target included in their bonus plan and performance contract
Strategy.option_d: Yes, all/most of our leadership have Sustainability targets in their bonus plans and performance contracts
Strategy.option_e: Yes, all members of our senior IT leadership team have SMART (or similar) Sustainability targets included in their bonus plans & performance contracts

Strategy.text: Do you have a Sustainability manager role/team in your IT organisation?
Strategy.option_a: No, we don't have anyone like that
Strategy.option_b: We have a Sustainability representative in IT, but they are relatively junior
Strategy.option_c: Yes, we have appointed a Sustainability officer/manager but they sit outside of IT
Strategy.option_d: Yes, we have a Sustainability manager who is building their team
Strategy.option_e: Yes, we have a Sustainability team in IT/digital services who own our Sustainability strategy and roadmap

Strategy.text: How much visibility into GHG emissions do your CIO/CTO and their teams receive?
Strategy.option_b: They receive an annual compliance report
Strategy.option_c: They receive regular high level quarterly updates
Strategy.option_d: They get regular detailed monthly/quarterly reports and have access to more details if they require
Strategy.option_e: GHG emissions are one of their key metrics and they receive detailed update on a monthly basis split into scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions

Strategy.text: Do you have a defined and agreed budget for Sustainability in IT/digital services?
Strategy.option_a: No, we don't have any budget
Strategy.option_b: We assign budget for Sustainability projects on ad hoc basis
Strategy.option_c: We have a budget, but it is not sufficient
Strategy.option_d: Yes, we have a budget for all Sustainability initiatives in IT, but it requires more rigour in understanding how it is spent
Strategy.option_e: Yes, we have a defined and agreed budget for the execution of our Sustainability roadmap in IT, which is aligned with targets and monitored

Strategy.text: Do you require your IT suppliers to provide you with their Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions and their calculations?
Strategy.option_b: We don't right now but we're working on defining the right policy to require our suppliers to share their emissions with us
Strategy.option_c: Yes, we require our suppliers to send us their Scope 1 and 2 emissions
Strategy.option_d: Yes, we require our suppliers to share with us their Scope 1 and 2 emissions and we are expanding it to include Scope 3 in the (near) future
Strategy.option_e: Yes, we require all our IT suppliers to provide us with as detailed emission calculations as possible, including Scope 3

Strategy.text: Do you monitor your IT vendors/suppliers Sustainability performance?
Strategy.option_b: Not right now but we're planning to monitor it in the (near) future
Strategy.option_c: Yes, we ask for their Sustainability performance data when they are onboarded
Strategy.option_d: Yes, our suppliers provide Sustainability performance data when onboarded and during any subsequent RFP/tender process
Strategy.option_e: Yes, our suppliers provide us with their Sustainability performance once a year, we review it with them and expect them to make progress

Strategy.text: Do you have an inventory for environmental impact that includes GHG emissions?
Strategy.option_b: We have a few local databases that include the environmental impact information
Strategy.option_c: Yes, we have a central database with some environmental impact information
Strategy.option_d: Yes, we have an environmental impact inventory, which is updated manually once a year
Strategy.option_e: Yes, we have a dedicated inventory that is automatically updated with the most up-to-date environmental impact information

Strategy.text: How detailed and extensive is your Scope 3 emissions calculation?
Strategy.option_b: We calculate them at high level, using high level assumptions and emissions factors
Strategy.option_c: We have tried to calculate Scope 3 of some of our products' supply chain and we're planning to do more
Strategy.option_d: We have identified the most relevant Scope 3 emissions and calculate them for our supply chain
Strategy.option_e: We hire an external party to conduct a full life-cycle assessment of most of our products and services, including Scope 3 emissions

Strategy.text: What communication and engagement activities do you do around Sustainability across IT?
Strategy.option_b: We tend to include an aspect related to Sustainability in our regular IT events and updates
Strategy.option_c: We share updates on Sustainability and are planning to expand our agenda of Sustainability related events
Strategy.option_d: We organise several Sustainability events per year depending on the need and always include Sustainability in internal IT communication
Strategy.option_e: We have a fairly detailed plan of various Sustainability events (meetings, town-halls, etc) at different levels of the organisation

Governance & Management.text: Who owns Sustainability governance for IT (EUS, cloud, hardware, software, procurement)?
Governance & Management.option_d: Formal role or committee within IT accountable for Sustainability
Governance & Management.option_e: Dedicated IT Sustainability governance board with CxO oversight, aligned to enterprise ESG strategy

Governance & Management.text: How is accountability structured across IT and business functions?
Governance & Management.option_c: Partial alignment between IT, procurement, and ESG
Governance & Management.option_d: Cross-functional working group with defined roles
Governance & Management.option_e: RACI structure with clear accountability, escalation paths, and board-level reporting

Governance & Management.text: Do you have formal Sustainability policies or standards for IT?
Governance & Management.option_d: Documented IT Sustainability standards (refresh, procurement, development)
Governance & Management.option_e: Comprehensive IT Sustainability policy aligned with ISO/GBS/EPEAT/CSRD, audited regularly

Governance & Management.text: How do you manage Sustainability budgeting in IT?
Governance & Management.option_c: Some Sustainability cost estimates in IT budgets
Governance & Management.option_d: Dedicated line items for Sustainability projects
Governance & Management.option_e: Integrated multi-year budgets across IT, with ROI and cost–emissions forecasting

Governance & Management.text: Do you forecast IT Sustainability impacts (cost, energy, emissions)?
Governance & Management.option_d: Regular forecasts combining cost and emissions
Governance & Management.option_e: Integrated into TBM/FinOps/GreenOps with scenario analysis (refresh vs refurb, supplier A vs B)

Governance & Management.text: Do you benchmark IT Sustainability performance?
Governance & Management.option_d: Regular benchmarking against industry frameworks (EU CoC, SBTi, Gartner)
Governance & Management.option_e: Continuous benchmarking, results disclosed externally (e.g. CDP, ESG reports)

Governance & Management.text: How are best practices and frameworks adopted?
Governance & Management.option_d: Broad adoption (ISO 30134, UK GBS, CSRD, EU CoC)
Governance & Management.option_e: Fully embedded in standards, procurement, contracts, and reporting

Governance & Management.text: How are risks of inaction on IT Sustainability assessed?
Governance & Management.option_c: Some risks tracked in IT registers
Governance & Management.option_d: Formal Sustainability risks included in enterprise risk management
Governance & Management.option_e: Full IT risk register includes Sustainability, linked to enterprise compliance frameworks

Governance & Management.text: How do you ensure continuous improvement?
Governance & Management.option_c: Annual reviews of policies and performance
Governance & Management.option_d: Continuous improvement programme with KPIs and feedback loops
Governance & Management.option_e: Embedded culture of improvement with independent audits and annual targets

Governance & Management.text: How is Sustainability performance reported?
Governance & Management.option_d: Quarterly KPI reports shared across IT, ESG, and leadership
Governance & Management.option_e: Continuous dashboards feeding board/executive reports, disclosed externally

Governance & Management.text: How would you characterise your organisation's operating posture on IT sustainability — are you primarily reporting consumption, actively optimising it, or systematically avoiding unnecessary consumption before it occurs?
Governance & Management.option_a: We are primarily in a reporting posture — we track and disclose what we consume, but this data does not systematically drive operational decisions or behaviour change
Governance & Management.option_b: We are beginning to move from reporting to optimisation in some domains — consumption data is used to identify inefficiencies and some improvement initiatives are underway, but coverage is inconsistent
Governance & Management.option_c: We are in an active optimisation posture across most IT domains — efficiency improvements are tracked, targets are set, and consumption data drives regular operational decisions; demand avoidance is discussed but not yet systematic
Governance & Management.option_d: We operate in both optimisation and early avoidance modes — we not only optimise what we run but actively challenge whether resources should be provisioned at all; demand governance, rightsizing, and capacity management are established disciplines
Governance & Management.option_e: Demand avoidance is our primary sustainability posture — we systematically challenge consumption before it occurs through demand governance, capacity management, and business unit accountability; optimisation is continuous and automated; reporting is an output of operational discipline, not its purpose

Governance & Management.text: How aligned is IT Sustainability governance with corporate climate goals (Net Zero, SBTi, CSR)?
Governance & Management.option_d: Aligned with corporate targets at high level
Governance & Management.option_e: Fully embedded — IT roadmaps directly support corporate climate/SBTi pathways

Governance & Management.text: What is the quality of sustainability KPIs tied to IT roles — do they measure efficiency and avoidance outcomes, or only total consumption?
Governance & Management.option_a: No sustainability KPIs are tied to IT roles
Governance & Management.option_b: Sustainability KPIs exist for some roles but are expressed only as absolute consumption or emissions totals, which can be met without genuine efficiency improvement
Governance & Management.option_c: KPIs include a mix of absolute and efficiency metrics, though intensity or avoidance targets are limited to a few domains and not consistently applied across IT leadership
Governance & Management.option_d: KPIs for IT leadership include defined efficiency metrics — consumption per unit of output, utilisation ratios, demand reduction targets — alongside absolute figures, reviewed in performance conversations
Governance & Management.option_e: IT leadership is held to a balanced set of KPIs covering absolute reduction, efficiency improvement, and demand avoidance; targets are intensity-based where possible, independently validated, and directly linked to compensation; the framework is reviewed annually and evolves as ambition increases

Governance & Management.text: Do you run change management and training for GreenOps adoption?
Governance & Management.option_d: Structured training and adoption programmes for IT teams
Governance & Management.option_e: Formal change management plan with mandatory training, user engagement, and behavioural nudges

Governance & Management.text: Who governs Sustainability data quality and assurance?
Governance & Management.option_d: Clear data ownership + internal audit/QA
Governance & Management.option_e: Independent assurance, confidence levels disclosed, third-party verification (e.g. limited/reasonable assurance)

Governance & Management.text: Do Sustainability criteria act as decision gates in IT governance?
Governance & Management.option_d: Mandatory stage gate for major IT projects
Governance & Management.option_e: Can block or reshape projects; Sustainability impact assessed alongside cost/security/performance

Governance & Management.text: How do you engage externally on IT Sustainability governance?
Governance & Management.option_d: Active in industry standards bodies (e.g. EU CoC, TBM/FinOps/GreenOps groups)
Governance & Management.option_e: Lead role in industry consortia, influencing regulations and shaping supplier behaviour

Governance & Management.text: Is Sustainability considered in IT programme/portfolio planning?
Governance & Management.option_b: Mentioned at portfolio level but no impact on decisions
Governance & Management.option_d: Regularly factored into portfolio planning alongside cost and risk
Governance & Management.option_e: Mandatory criterion in programme prioritisation, with quantified cost–emissions trade-offs

Governance & Management.text: How mature is your IT capacity management practice — does it actively match provisioned resource to actual and forecast demand across your estate, with sustainability as an explicit consideration?
Governance & Management.option_a: Capacity management is reactive — resources are provisioned when requested and rarely reviewed; over-provisioning is the norm and its sustainability implications are not considered
Governance & Management.option_b: Some capacity planning occurs for major infrastructure investments, but it is driven by performance and cost rather than demand evidence; sustainability is not a consideration
Governance & Management.option_c: Capacity management processes exist for some domains and consider utilisation data, but coverage is incomplete, reviews are infrequent, and sustainability is a secondary factor at best
Governance & Management.option_d: Formal capacity management is practised across major IT domains — provisioned resource is regularly reviewed against actual and forecast demand, rightsizing recommendations are acted upon, and sustainability impact is an explicit output of capacity planning decisions
Governance & Management.option_e: Capacity management is a mature, cross-domain governance discipline: provisioned resource is continuously matched to demand using automated tooling; business units are required to justify capacity requests against evidence; sustainability intensity — consumption per unit of output — is a formal output of every capacity review; and capacity decisions are linked to budget, operational, and sustainability governance in a single integrated process

Governance & Management.text: Do architecture boards apply Sustainability stage gates?
Governance & Management.option_d: Consistently used as stage gates in reviews
Governance & Management.option_e: Mandatory Sustainability gate in all architecture/governance boards

Governance & Management.text: Are design principles for IT architecture sustainability-aware?
Governance & Management.option_c: Draft principles exist but inconsistently applied
Governance & Management.option_d: Formal architecture principles include energy efficiency, circularity, carbon impact
Governance & Management.option_e: Sustainability principles fully embedded, aligned to enterprise/industry standards (e.g. ISO 30134, EU CoC)

Governance & Management.text: Are trade-offs between performance, cost, and emissions assessed in design?
Governance & Management.option_d: Quantified trade-offs included in all solution designs
Governance & Management.option_e: Trade-offs mandatory, with executive-level approval for high-emission choices

Governance & Management.text: How is Sustainability risk tracked in project/programme delivery?
Governance & Management.option_d: Standard risk tracking in all projects
Governance & Management.option_e: Linked to enterprise risk management and board reporting

Financial & Service Management.text: Do you have a defined IT cost allocation model (services, portfolios, business units, environments)?
Financial & Service Management.option_a: No allocation model; IT spend tracked only at aggregate level.
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Partial allocation exists by department or tower, but inconsistent.
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Service-based costing is defined and applied across much of IT. Allocations are visible but not used systematically.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Allocation is granular across services, sourcing models, and environments, reconciled with finance.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Allocation is workload- and BU-specific, reconciled with sustainability metrics, and independently validated.

Financial & Service Management.text: How consistently are allocation methods applied across the enterprise?
Financial & Service Management.option_a: No consistency; each team reports differently.
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Some allocation rules exist but are applied unevenly.
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Defined standards exist and are applied in most areas, though exceptions remain.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Allocation rules are enforced enterprise-wide with regular review.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Methods are benchmarked externally and recognised as best practice.

Financial & Service Management.text: How are budgets allocated across IT services, portfolios, and sourcing models?
Financial & Service Management.option_a: Budgets are set at aggregate IT level.
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Budgets are partially broken down by department or tower.
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Budgets are allocated to services/portfolios but rarely tied to demand or sustainability.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Budgets are granular and linked to demand, sourcing choices, and sustainability priorities.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Budgets are dynamic, scenario-based, and directly linked to service sustainability outcomes.

Financial & Service Management.text: How are budgets reforecast to reflect demand or sourcing changes?
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Reforecasting occurs ad hoc when major variances arise.
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Reforecasting is scheduled but focuses only on cost.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Reforecasting is automated and considers sourcing and demand shifts.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Forecasting integrates financial and sustainability scenarios dynamically, informing governance decisions.

Financial & Service Management.text: How are forecasts created and reviewed?
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Forecasts use run-rates, with low accuracy.
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Forecasting is systematic, reviewed quarterly, with improving accuracy.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Forecasts are automated, scenario-based, and reconciled with portfolio and sourcing strategies.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Forecasts integrate carbon, energy, and cost; results are used at board level for strategic decision-making.

Financial & Service Management.text: To what extent do forecasts include scenario modelling for sustainability?
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Informal scenarios are run occasionally, with limited influence.
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Sustainability scenarios are included in some forecasts.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Forecasting routinely incorporates scenarios such as workload placement, sourcing, or refresh cycles.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Sustainability scenarios are automated and embedded in portfolio and sourcing strategy.

Financial & Service Management.text: How are sustainability metrics integrated into financial models?
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Mentioned in reports but not embedded.
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Linked to selected cost centres (cloud, data centre).
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Fully reconciled with cost and performance data, reviewed in governance forums.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Sustainability metrics are embedded in allocation, budgeting, and forecasting, externally validated.

Financial & Service Management.text: What is the frequency and granularity of cost and sustainability reporting?
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Reports produced quarterly, inconsistent in detail.
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Reports produced monthly, with breakdowns by service or tower.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Reports automated and reconciled with finance; cover cost, performance, and sustainability at service level.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Reports are near real-time, granular to workload, externally assured, and disclosed publicly.

Financial & Service Management.text: How are reports tailored for different stakeholders (CIO, CFO, ESG, BU leaders)?
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Some tailoring occurs but outputs are inconsistent.
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Reports are produced for multiple audiences, but formats vary.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Reports are tailored to each stakeholder group, consistent and automated.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Reports are integrated into decision workflows, informing both internal stakeholders and external disclosure.

Financial & Service Management.text: To what extent are reporting processes automated and reconciled across functions?
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Automated for core services, reconciled with finance but not ESG data.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Enterprise-wide automation reconciles finance, ops, and sustainability data.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Fully automated, independently validated, and used for regulatory and investor disclosure.

Financial & Service Management.text: How mature are your defined TBM/FinOps methods and practices?
Financial & Service Management.option_c: TBM/FinOps methods defined and applied across most services.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Methods embedded enterprise-wide, with oversight and training.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Practices benchmarked externally, continuously improved, and used to shape industry standards.

Financial & Service Management.text: How is financial and sustainability data used in governance decisions?
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Data presented in governance forums but not acted upon.
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Data influences some sourcing and demand decisions.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Integrated cost and sustainability reports guide investment, demand, and sourcing.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Governance is consistently driven by integrated models; organisation influences industry standards.

Financial & Service Management.text: Does sustainability efficiency data — not just cost data — actively influence IT budget decisions, investment prioritisation, and resource allocation?
Financial & Service Management.option_a: Sustainability data is not used in budget decisions — financial decisions are based on cost and service performance only
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Sustainability data is occasionally referenced in budget discussions but does not systematically influence outcomes; cost remains the dominant decision criterion
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Sustainability efficiency data is presented alongside financial data in some budget reviews and occasionally influences prioritisation decisions, particularly for major capital investments
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Sustainability efficiency metrics are a formal input to IT budget and investment decisions — inefficient assets or services can be de-prioritised or redesigned on sustainability grounds, and efficiency improvement targets inform budget envelopes
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Sustainability efficiency is fully integrated into the financial decision framework: budget allocations explicitly reward efficiency improvement and penalise waste; consumption intensity targets are set at business-unit level alongside financial targets; and the financial model is structured to incentivise avoidance of unnecessary consumption, not just its accurate reporting

Financial & Service Management.text: Does your IT financial model use chargeback or showback to make business units accountable for their IT consumption — including its sustainability cost — in ways that change their behaviour?
Financial & Service Management.option_a: IT costs are pooled and charged to business units as a fixed overhead; there is no consumption-based accountability and business units have no visibility of or incentive to reduce their IT footprint
Financial & Service Management.option_b: Showback exists — business units can see their approximate IT consumption and associated costs — but there are no financial consequences and consumption behaviour is not materially affected
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Partial chargeback exists for some IT services; business units see their consumption costs but sustainability impact is not part of the charge; behavioural change is limited
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Chargeback or consumption-based allocation covers most significant IT services; business units are financially accountable for their consumption; sustainability costs are visible alongside financial costs and are beginning to influence demand decisions
Financial & Service Management.option_e: A mature consumption accountability model is in place: business units are charged for the full cost of their IT consumption including its sustainability impact; the model is granular enough to incentivise efficient behaviour; units that reduce consumption intensity are rewarded; and the combined financial and sustainability accountability model is reviewed annually and has demonstrably changed business unit behaviour

Financial & Service Management.text: How do IT, finance, procurement, and sustainability functions collaborate?
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Cross-functional working groups exist, though maturity varies.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Governance forums review cost and sustainability data with cross-functional accountability.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Collaboration is embedded, with shared KPIs and joint ownership of outcomes.

Financial & Service Management.text: Do governance forums include joint accountability for cost and sustainability outcomes?
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Partial accountability included in charters or performance reviews.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Clear joint accountability across IT, finance, and ESG leaders.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Shared KPIs cascade across functions; incentives aligned with financial and sustainability outcomes.

Financial & Service Management.text: How integrated are financial and sustainability systems and tooling?
Financial & Service Management.option_c: Partial integration between finance and IT tooling, but ESG data separate.
Financial & Service Management.option_d: Fully integrated across TBM/FinOps, finance, and ESG platforms.
Financial & Service Management.option_e: Systems provide a single source of truth, with advanced analytics and benchmarking.

Supply Chain.text: How is Sustainability weighted in procurement decisions?
Supply Chain.option_c: Considered at corporate level (supplier ESG ratings)
Supply Chain.option_d: Explicit weighting in tenders (10–20%), includes product-level criteria
Supply Chain.option_e: High weighting (>30%) with mandatory product-level LCA/PCF, circularity and emissions thresholds

Supply Chain.text: At what level do you assess supplier Sustainability?
Supply Chain.option_c: Corporate + some product eco-labels (e.g. EnergyStar, TCO)

Data Centre.text: Do you measure data centre sustainability performance using intensity or efficiency metrics — consumption per unit of IT output — rather than solely tracking absolute energy and emissions figures?
Data Centre.option_a: We track only absolute figures — total energy, total emissions, total water — with no normalisation against IT output or business value delivered
Data Centre.option_b: We use standard infrastructure efficiency ratios such as PUE but do not normalise consumption against IT output or compute delivered; we cannot distinguish genuine efficiency improvement from estate size changes

Data Centre.option_d: Defined intensity metrics are tracked alongside absolute figures — covering consumption per rack unit, per kW of IT load, or equivalent — with targets set, trends reviewed regularly, and results reported alongside PUE and absolute consumption in governance forums
Data Centre.option_e: Comprehensive efficiency intensity metrics are the primary lens for data centre sustainability performance: consumption per unit of compute, per transaction served, or per unit of business output is defined, tracked continuously, benchmarked externally, and used as the basis for investment decisions, capacity planning, and leadership accountability — absolute figures are a secondary view

Data Centre.text: How mature is your data centre capacity management process — does it actively challenge new capacity requests and match provisioned capacity to actual and forecast demand?

Data Centre.option_c: Capacity management processes exist and consider utilisation data, but new capacity requests are rarely challenged on sustainability grounds and the process does not systematically distinguish between genuine demand growth and over-provisioning
Data Centre.option_d: Formal capacity management is in place: provisioned capacity is reviewed against actual utilisation and forecast demand regularly; new capacity requests require evidence-based justification; consolidation and decommissioning are active disciplines; and sustainability impact is an explicit output of capacity planning decisions
Data Centre.option_e: Capacity management is a strategic discipline: every capacity request is assessed against demand evidence, efficiency alternatives, and sustainability impact before approval; utilisation thresholds trigger automatic review; the organisation has a demonstrated track record of avoiding capacity expansion through consolidation, virtualisation, and demand management; and capacity planning outcomes are reported in sustainability and financial governance forums as evidence of avoidance activity

Data Centre.option_e: We calculate scope 3 emissions ourselves using information provided by suppliers/data collected by ourselves and have this externally validated
