AI & Advanced Compute.text: Do you consider water consumption and cooling impacts when deploying or procuring AI workloads?
AI & Advanced Compute.option_a: Water consumption is not considered in any AI-related decision
AI & Advanced Compute.option_b: We are aware that AI workloads have significant cooling and water impacts but have no data to quantify them
AI & Advanced Compute.option_c: We use provider-level WUE estimates to give an approximate picture, but workload-level data is not available
AI & Advanced Compute.option_d: Water and cooling impacts are tracked at a workload or facility level and included in regular sustainability reporting
AI & Advanced Compute.option_e: Water consumption is a formal criterion in AI workload placement decisions; real-time or near-real-time data feeds into optimisation alongside energy and carbon metrics

AI & Advanced Compute.text: How is AI model training justified and approved in terms of cost, performance, and environmental impact?
AI & Advanced Compute.option_a: Training decisions are made informally by technical teams with no structured justification
AI & Advanced Compute.option_b: Business-led approval exists but is driven by capability or cost considerations only
AI & Advanced Compute.option_c: A basic business case is required, covering cost and intended performance outcomes, but environmental impact is not included
AI & Advanced Compute.option_d: Training requires a structured assessment covering cost, performance, and sustainability impact, with alternatives such as fine-tuning or model reuse explicitly evaluated
AI & Advanced Compute.option_e: Executive sign-off is required for significant training workloads, with quantified cost–carbon trade-offs presented; decisions are logged and reviewed as part of ongoing AI governance

AI & Advanced Compute.text: Do you optimise AI models for efficiency — through model selection, size, distillation, or inference architecture?
AI & Advanced Compute.option_a: Model efficiency is not a consideration; the most capable available model is typically chosen
AI & Advanced Compute.option_b: Basic guidance exists on prompt efficiency or context window management, but no systematic approach to model selection or architecture
AI & Advanced Compute.option_c: Efficiency is considered on an ad-hoc basis, with some teams experimenting with smaller models or quantisation
AI & Advanced Compute.option_d: Efficiency-first model selection is standard practice: smaller models, distillation, transfer learning, and inference reuse are applied where appropriate, with sustainability as a stated rationale
AI & Advanced Compute.option_e: An organisation-wide efficiency strategy governs model selection, architecture, and deployment — models are selected and optimised to minimise emissions across their full operational lifecycle, with outcomes tracked and reported

AI & Advanced Compute.text: How do you manage the physical lifecycle of AI hardware (GPUs, TPUs, accelerators)?
AI & Advanced Compute.option_a: AI hardware lifecycle is not distinguished from general server refresh — no specific consideration of embodied carbon or end-of-life
AI & Advanced Compute.option_b: We rely on vendor reporting for hardware specifications but do not track embodied carbon or manage AI hardware specifically
AI & Advanced Compute.option_c: Operational energy consumption of AI hardware is monitored, but embodied carbon and end-of-life treatment are not formally managed
AI & Advanced Compute.option_d: Embodied and operational emissions of AI hardware are tracked, and lifecycle decisions — including refresh timing, redeployment, and resale — consider sustainability alongside performance and cost
AI & Advanced Compute.option_e: Full lifecycle carbon footprint (PCF/LCA) of AI hardware is maintained, linked to ITAM and GreenOps reporting, with reuse, resale, and responsible disposal managed to defined standards

AI & Advanced Compute.text: How do you decide where AI workloads run — across cloud, private infrastructure, and HPC?
AI & Advanced Compute.option_a: Placement decisions are driven by what is technically available or easiest to procure, with no structured criteria
AI & Advanced Compute.option_b: Cost and performance are the primary placement criteria; sustainability is not considered
AI & Advanced Compute.option_c: Sustainability factors are considered informally in some workload placement decisions, but there is no consistent framework
AI & Advanced Compute.option_d: A structured placement framework includes sustainability alongside cost, performance, and security — applied consistently for significant workloads
AI & Advanced Compute.option_e: Workload placement is optimised across cost, carbon, water intensity, grid carbon, and regulatory constraints in near real time; placement decisions are reviewed regularly against evolving infrastructure and grid data

AI & Advanced Compute.text: How do you evaluate and hold AI providers and model vendors accountable for sustainability performance?
AI & Advanced Compute.option_a: Provider sustainability is not assessed; procurement is based on capability and commercial terms
AI & Advanced Compute.option_b: We review publicly available sustainability statements but do not verify claims or include sustainability in commercial negotiations
AI & Advanced Compute.option_c: Sustainability criteria are included in procurement evaluations for major AI services, including consideration of published model cards and energy disclosures
AI & Advanced Compute.option_d: Providers are required to supply verified energy and emissions data as part of contract terms; performance is reviewed at least annually against agreed standards
AI & Advanced Compute.option_e: Sustainability requirements are embedded in AI provider contracts with measurable commitments; performance is monitored continuously, and poor performance informs renewal and sourcing decisions

AI & Advanced Compute.text: How is carbon from AI workloads attributed to the business units or products that consume them?
AI & Advanced Compute.option_a: AI-related carbon is not attributed — it sits as an unallocated infrastructure cost
AI & Advanced Compute.option_b: High-level attribution exists at department or cost-centre level, based on broad estimates
AI & Advanced Compute.option_c: Attribution is made to teams or projects based on compute consumption, though methods are inconsistent and not reconciled with financial data
AI & Advanced Compute.option_d: AI carbon is attributed to business units using consistent methodology, integrated with FinOps or TBM tooling, and reviewed in governance forums
AI & Advanced Compute.option_e: Real-time or near-real-time carbon attribution is available at business-unit, product, or project level; data is used to drive consumption decisions, chargeback models, and executive reporting

AI & Advanced Compute.text: How aligned is AI sustainability governance with your broader AI ethics, risk, and responsible AI framework?
AI & Advanced Compute.option_a: AI sustainability and AI ethics/governance operate entirely separately with no coordination
AI & Advanced Compute.option_b: Both exist as distinct policies but there is no formal integration or shared governance structure
AI & Advanced Compute.option_c: Some overlap exists — sustainability is referenced in AI ethics policies or vice versa — but governance processes remain separate
AI & Advanced Compute.option_d: Formal alignment has been established between AI ethics, risk, and sustainability governance, with shared oversight mechanisms and coordinated reporting
AI & Advanced Compute.option_e: A unified AI governance framework covers ethics, risk, responsible use, and sustainability as interconnected dimensions; decisions in any one area are assessed for their implications across all others

AI & Advanced Compute.text: How is AI Sustainability data integrated into your FinOps, TBM, and GreenOps reporting?
AI & Advanced Compute.option_a: AI sustainability data sits in isolation — it is not connected to financial, operational, or GreenOps reporting
AI & Advanced Compute.option_b: Some AI metrics are tracked manually in separate tools or spreadsheets, but they are not reconciled with other reporting
AI & Advanced Compute.option_c: Selected AI sustainability data is included in broader GreenOps or FinOps reporting, but coverage is incomplete and methods vary
AI & Advanced Compute.option_d: AI energy, carbon, and cost data are integrated into GreenOps and FinOps dashboards with consistent methodology, reviewed alongside other infrastructure domains
AI & Advanced Compute.option_e: AI sustainability metrics are fully integrated in real time across TBM, FinOps, and GreenOps platforms; data is used actively for allocation, optimisation decisions, and executive and regulatory reporting

AI & Advanced Compute.text: How granularly do you attribute the carbon and energy cost of AI usage — at the model, job, or inference level?
AI & Advanced Compute.option_a: We have no visibility of AI energy or carbon consumption below the level of total infrastructure spend — AI workloads are not distinguished from general compute
AI & Advanced Compute.option_b: We can estimate AI-related energy use at a cluster or platform level, but cannot break this down by model, workload, or usage type
AI & Advanced Compute.option_c: Attribution is possible at the model or deployment level for major workloads — we know roughly what a given model costs to run — but per-job or per-query granularity is not available
AI & Advanced Compute.option_d: We track energy and carbon consumption at the job or run level, enabling us to compare the cost of individual training runs, batch inference jobs, or deployment configurations against their business value
AI & Advanced Compute.option_e: We operate a per-inference or per-token attribution model for production AI services — the carbon and energy cost of serving a query or generating an output is known, allocated to the consuming product or business unit, and used to inform model selection, caching strategy, and efficiency investment

Cloud Ops.text: Who owns the Sustainability agenda within your cloud operations team?
Cloud Ops.option_a: We don't have a specific Sustainability agenda in the team, we assume that is the vendor's responsibility
Cloud Ops.option_c: The Sustainability team own the agenda and liaise with senior management & the cloud operations teams
Cloud Ops.option_d: The cloud team owns the agenda
Cloud Ops.option_e: The agenda is split across multiple teams (Sustainability, senior management & cloud operations)

Cloud Ops.text: How are your cloud decisions aligned with wider IT efficiency and Sustainability goals?
Cloud Ops.option_a: We take a performance first approach, Sustainability doesn't enter into our decision making
Cloud Ops.option_b: We take a performance first approach. Retrospectively we do then consider sustainability, but it is not mandatory
Cloud Ops.option_c: We take a performance first approach, Sustainability is a secondary criteria
Cloud Ops.option_d: Sustainability is an important factor in team decision making, and our decisions often align with Sustainability goals
Cloud Ops.option_e: Sustainability is a critical factor in decision making. Standard processes ensure alignment with Sustainability goals

Cloud Ops.text: Do you have a FinOps team/resource that is focused on optimising and reporting on your cloud usage, including implementing cost savings initiatives?
Cloud Ops.option_a: No, we don't have a FinOps team
Cloud Ops.option_b: We have someone who periodically looks at cloud usage but it is not a defined role
Cloud Ops.option_c: Yes, we have someone allocated and have identified some savings
Cloud Ops.option_d: Yes, we have a structured FinOps process and have implemented some savings but do not have defined KPIs or targets
Cloud Ops.option_e: Yes, our FinOps function is very mature and embedded into the wider organisation. We regularly report on cloud usage trends and have defined KPIs and targets

Cloud Ops.text: How systematically do you identify and eliminate idle, zombie, and oversized cloud resources — treating avoidance of unnecessary consumption as a first-order discipline rather than a periodic optimisation exercise?
Cloud Ops.option_a: Idle and oversized resources are not systematically identified; resource sprawl accumulates and is addressed only when cost alerts are triggered
Cloud Ops.option_b: Periodic rightsizing reviews occur, typically driven by cost pressure rather than a sustainability agenda; zombie resources are identified and removed on an ad-hoc basis
Cloud Ops.option_c: Scheduled reviews identify idle and oversized resources across major cloud environments; some automation exists for alerting, but remediation relies on manual intervention and coverage is incomplete
Cloud Ops.option_d: Automated tooling continuously identifies idle, oversized, and untagged resources across the cloud estate; remediation is triggered within defined SLAs; avoidance of unnecessary resource creation is a defined engineering expectation enforced through review gates
Cloud Ops.option_e: Cloud resource avoidance is a first-order operational discipline: all resource creation requires justified business need and defined lifecycle; automated governance continuously eliminates idle and oversized consumption; avoided consumption is quantified in both cost and carbon terms and reported to leadership as a primary sustainability outcome alongside absolute consumption figures

Cloud Ops.text: Do you have a budget specifically committed to Sustainability initiatives?
Cloud Ops.option_a: No we don't have a specific Sustainability budget inside the team
Cloud Ops.option_b: Not really, though we have basic budget allocated for FinOps tooling to optimise usage
Cloud Ops.option_c: No, there is an overall budget, which includes miscellaneous items such as Sustainability
Cloud Ops.option_d: Yes we do have a Sustainability budget, but it has not grown over the last 5 years
Cloud Ops.option_e: Yes we do have a Sustainability budget, and it's grown year on year

Cloud Ops.text: Do you regularly review cloud usage to assess adherence to company carbon reduction targets & policies?
Cloud Ops.option_a: No, cloud usage is not reviewed from the context of carbon reduction targets
Cloud Ops.option_b: Yes, but only at a basic level on an annual basis using vendor provided metrics at an account level
Cloud Ops.option_c: Yes, we track usage on a quarterly basis using vendor provided emissions metrics at an account level
Cloud Ops.option_d: Yes, usage and estimated emissions are tracked at a granular level and reviewed monthly with a team/resource focussed on identifying waste
Cloud Ops.option_e: Yes, usage and calculated scope 1, 2 & 3 metrics are tracked in our automated tooling at a granular level, reviewed monthly with a proven process for identification and remediation of waste with agreed KPIs and targets

Cloud Ops.text: Do you actively benchmark your cloud providers with regards to Sustainability?
Cloud Ops.option_a: No, we do not benchmark our providers
Cloud Ops.option_b: Yes, we manually review their available information on an annual basis
Cloud Ops.option_c: Yes, we manually review their available information on an bi-annual basis
Cloud Ops.option_d: Yes, we review internally and validate with external 3rd parties on a quarterly basis, including tracking their adherence with their carbon reduction programme
Cloud Ops.option_e: Yes, we review internally and validate with external 3rd parties on a monthly basis, including tracking their adherence with their carbon reduction programme

Cloud Ops.text: How do you monitor GHG emissions and energy consumption of your cloud services?
Cloud Ops.option_a: We do not track energy consumption or carbon emissions for cloud services
Cloud Ops.option_b: We are unable to accurately track energy consumption as the data is not made available but use the basic information provided by our cloud vendor for carbon emissions
Cloud Ops.option_c: We manually gather vendor provided metrics for carbon emissions on a monthly basis and have our own estimates for energy consumption
Cloud Ops.option_d: We use vendor tooling that tracks scope 1 & 2 carbon emissions from our cloud vendors in near real time
Cloud Ops.option_e: We use externally validated tooling that tracks scope 1, 2 & 3 emissions from our cloud vendors in near real time

Cloud Ops.text: How do you calculate and track the GHG emissions of your cloud (Scope 3)?
Cloud Ops.option_a: We do not track Scope 3 emissions from our cloud providers
Cloud Ops.option_b: We use estimates from the provider
Cloud Ops.option_c: We use the vendor provided tooling to track Scope 3 estimates at an account level on a quarterly basis
Cloud Ops.option_d: We use vendor provided and/or independent tooling to proactively track Scope 3 emissions on a monthly basis at an account level
Cloud Ops.option_e: We use vendor provided and/or independent tooling to proactively track Scope 3 emissions and feed this into our FinOps team with agreed KPIs and targets

Cloud Ops.text: How frequently do you calculate the avoided GHG emissions of your cloud services?
Cloud Ops.option_a: We do not calculate avoided GHG emissions
Cloud Ops.option_d: Monthly - with a manual process that is externally validated
Cloud Ops.option_e: In near real time, via an automated process and externally validated calculations

Cloud Ops.text: How frequently do you update your environmental impact & GHG inventory with cloud Sustainability data?
Cloud Ops.option_a: We do not update our GHG inventory with cloud data
Cloud Ops.option_b: Once a year, with manually gathered estimates
Cloud Ops.option_c: Once every 6-months, with manually gathered estimates
Cloud Ops.option_d: Once a quarter, using vendor metrics
Cloud Ops.option_e: Every month, it's an automated process

Cloud Ops.text: How do you govern cloud resource tagging and cost/emissions allocation to ensure data is attributable to business units, applications, and environments?
Cloud Ops.option_a: Tagging is inconsistent or absent — cloud spend and emissions cannot be reliably attributed to teams, applications, or environments
Cloud Ops.option_b: Basic tagging exists for some resources but coverage is incomplete; attribution is approximate and not reconciled with financial or sustainability reporting
Cloud Ops.option_c: A tagging policy exists and is applied to most new resources, but legacy resources are not fully tagged and allocation gaps remain
Cloud Ops.option_d: A comprehensive tagging standard is enforced across all cloud environments; allocation of cost and emissions to business units, applications, and environments is consistent and reconciled with financial data
Cloud Ops.option_e: Tagging governance is automated and continuously enforced; any untagged resource is flagged and remediated; full attribution of cost, carbon, and energy to business unit, product, and environment is available in real time and forms the basis for chargeback and sustainability reporting

Cloud Ops.text: Does your cloud consumption accountability model — through chargeback, showback, or equivalent — include sustainability costs in ways that actively incentivise business units to reduce unnecessary cloud consumption?
Cloud Ops.option_a: Business units have no visibility of their cloud consumption or its cost; IT absorbs all cloud spend centrally with no allocation
Cloud Ops.option_b: Showback exists — business units can see their approximate cloud consumption and cost — but sustainability cost is not included and there is no financial consequence for overconsumption
Cloud Ops.option_c: Chargeback covers cloud financial costs for most services; business units are financially accountable but sustainability impact is not part of the charge; the model has limited influence on avoidance behaviour
Cloud Ops.option_d: Chargeback includes both financial and sustainability costs for major cloud services; business units see the carbon and energy cost of their consumption alongside the financial charge; this is beginning to influence architectural and demand decisions
Cloud Ops.option_e: The cloud accountability model fully integrates financial and sustainability costs: business units are charged for the complete cost of consumption including its carbon and energy impact; efficient teams are rewarded through lower charges; the model has demonstrably changed business unit behaviour — architectural decisions, deployment patterns, and demand management have all shifted in response to sustainability-inclusive accountability

Cloud Ops.text: How often does your cloud team prepare and share Sustainability reports?
Cloud Ops.option_a: We don't prepare or share Sustainability reports
Cloud Ops.option_b: Reports are prepared annually but rarely shared
Cloud Ops.option_c: Reports are prepared annually and shared with the Sustainability team & senior management
Cloud Ops.option_d: Reports are prepared monthly and shared with the Sustainability team & senior management
Cloud Ops.option_e: The data is captured continuously and automatically fed into a Sustainability platform used by the Sustainability team & senior management

Cloud Ops.text: Do you review how your cloud providers are operating from a Sustainability perspective?
Cloud Ops.option_b: Yes, we use the information publicly available on their website when choosing vendors
Cloud Ops.option_c: Yes, we review the publicly available information and independent analyst reports on an annual basis
Cloud Ops.option_d: Yes, we review public information and require our vendors to present updates in our quarterly account review sessions
Cloud Ops.option_e: Yes, we use external independent analysts to review information provided by our vendors, assessing their adherence with their agreed targets

Cloud Ops.text: Is your cloud operations team(s) trained in the area of Sustainability?
Cloud Ops.option_a: No, they have not had any specific Sustainability related training
Cloud Ops.option_b: They are free to use the basic training tools and resources that we have available
Cloud Ops.option_c: Yes, we have groups & communities that serve the purpose of training and efficiency - Sustainability is part of it
Cloud Ops.option_d: Yes, our team members receive some standardised training how to design and operate cloud resources efficiently from the context of Sustainability
Cloud Ops.option_e: Yes, our team(s) is well trained to operate cloud efficiently and to minimise its impact on the environment. We have tools to measure efficiency, which are continuously used

Cloud Ops.text: Do your software development teams share projected workload characteristics with you when deploying into the Cloud?
Cloud Ops.option_a: No, workload profiles are not shared, resources are built and deployed as documented
Cloud Ops.option_b: We have limited access but tend to build as per standards
Cloud Ops.option_c: We have done in the past and planning to do it more often in the future
Cloud Ops.option_d: Yes, from time to time we discuss how to best optimise workloads and cloud resources
Cloud Ops.option_e: Yes, we regularly meet with our developers to understand how to optimise code development with the cloud resources

Software Development.text: Who owns the Sustainability agenda within your software development team(s)?
Software Development.option_a: No one owns it — sustainability is not a defined responsibility within any development team
Software Development.option_b: We have discussed appointing an owner and plan to do so, but no formal responsibility exists today
Software Development.option_c: A contact within the team liaises informally with the central Sustainability or IT function, but has no formal mandate or dedicated time
Software Development.option_d: A named team member owns the sustainability agenda, promotes it within the team, and has a defined working relationship with the business Sustainability function
Software Development.option_e: A dedicated role at manager level owns the sustainability agenda for software development, with formal authority, time allocation, and direct accountability to senior leadership

Software Development.text: Do you have a budget for Sustainability initiatives within your development team?
Software Development.option_a: No dedicated budget exists and it is not under active consideration
Software Development.option_b: We rely on the central Sustainability team to fund work when it arises — there is no development-owned budget
Software Development.option_c: Budgets are agreed on a project-by-project basis where sustainability objectives are included, but there is no standing allocation
Software Development.option_d: A defined, if modest, annual budget exists within the development function specifically for sustainability initiatives
Software Development.option_e: A dedicated annual sustainability budget is in place, sized to deliver a meaningful programme of work, and has grown as the agenda has matured

Software Development.text: Does your overarching strategy for software development consider sustainability?
Software Development.option_a: Our development strategy does not include sustainability, or we do not have a formal development strategy
Software Development.option_b: We intend to include environmental impact in our strategy but have not yet done so
Software Development.option_c: Sustainability is referenced at a high level in our development strategy but is not mandatory and has no associated metrics
Software Development.option_d: Sustainability is a defined component of our development strategy, with specific principles, metrics, and design considerations documented
Software Development.option_e: Sustainability is a core, non-negotiable element of our development strategy, fully aligned with the corporate sustainability strategy, with measurable outcomes and executive ownership

Software Development.text: Do you have formal environmental impact principles guiding design, development, and architecture decisions — and do you measure adherence to them?
Software Development.option_a: No principles exist and environmental impact is not considered in design or development decisions
Software Development.option_b: Environmental impact is discussed informally but there are no documented principles or measurement processes
Software Development.option_c: Principles are being developed or exist in draft form; some teams apply them but adoption is inconsistent and adherence is not tracked
Software Development.option_d: Formal principles are documented and applied in development processes, but adherence relies on individual commitment rather than automated enforcement or measurement
Software Development.option_e: Formal sustainability principles are embedded in development standards, applied consistently across teams, measured against defined targets, and reviewed regularly for effectiveness

Software Development.text: How do you monitor the energy consumption of your software in production?
Software Development.option_a: We do not monitor or track energy consumption attributable to our software
Software Development.option_b: We are exploring tooling options but have no measurement capability in place today
Software Development.option_c: We have run targeted projects to estimate energy consumption for specific applications, but this is not continuous or systematic
Software Development.option_d: We use tooling to monitor energy consumption for major production workloads, with results reviewed periodically and used to identify optimisation opportunities
Software Development.option_e: Automated tooling continuously monitors energy consumption across production software, with real-time data feeding into dashboards, targets set at application level, and results used to drive architectural and operational decisions

Software Development.text: How do you manage development and test environments to minimise unnecessary energy consumption?
Software Development.option_a: Development and test environments are provisioned on demand and left running — there is no active management of idle or unused environments
Software Development.option_b: We are aware that unmanaged environments waste energy but have no systematic process for controlling them
Software Development.option_c: Some controls exist — for example, policies on environment size or manual shutdown procedures — but compliance is inconsistent and enforcement is limited
Software Development.option_d: Environment lifecycle is actively managed: automated shutdown schedules, right-sizing standards, and environment usage policies are in place and largely followed
Software Development.option_e: Development and test environments are fully governed: ephemeral by default, right-sized by policy, automatically terminated when idle, and their energy consumption is tracked and attributed alongside production workloads

Software Development.text: How sustainable are your CI/CD pipelines and build processes?
Software Development.option_a: Pipeline efficiency and environmental impact are not considered — pipelines run as configured without review of energy or resource consumption
Software Development.option_b: We are aware that inefficient pipelines consume unnecessary compute but have not taken steps to measure or address this
Software Development.option_c: We have reviewed pipeline efficiency in some areas and made improvements, but there is no systematic approach and coverage is incomplete
Software Development.option_d: Pipeline efficiency is a defined engineering concern: build times, test suite optimisation, and unnecessary pipeline runs are monitored and regularly reviewed
Software Development.option_e: CI/CD pipelines are engineered and continuously optimised for efficiency: build caching, selective test execution, pipeline consolidation, and energy-aware scheduling are standard practice, and pipeline energy consumption is tracked as an operational metric

Software Development.text: Do developers make architecture and design choices that consider the downstream energy and resource efficiency of the software they build?
Software Development.option_a: Architecture decisions are driven by functionality, performance, and delivery speed — environmental efficiency is not a consideration
Software Development.option_b: Some developers are personally aware of efficiency implications in architecture choices but there is no organisational expectation or standard
Software Development.option_c: Sustainability is referenced in architecture guidance or non-functional requirements in some contexts, but application is inconsistent across teams and projects
Software Development.option_d: Sustainability is a defined non-functional requirement in architecture and design processes; decisions such as serverless vs always-on, data retention, and dependency selection are evaluated for efficiency implications
Software Development.option_e: Energy and resource efficiency are first-class architectural concerns: embedded in design standards, evaluated at architecture review gates, tracked through delivery, and optimised continuously in production

Software Development.text: How are Scope 3 emissions associated with software development activities understood and reported?
Software Development.option_a: Scope 3 emissions from development activities are not tracked or understood
Software Development.option_b: We understand conceptually that development activities generate Scope 3 emissions but do not have a methodology or data to calculate them
Software Development.option_c: A basic methodology exists for estimating Scope 3 emissions from development activities, using modelled or estimated data
Software Development.option_d: Scope 3 emissions from development are calculated using a consistent methodology with reasonably reliable data, and results are included in the organisation's GHG inventory
Software Development.option_e: Scope 3 emissions from software development are calculated using measured data, reported with confidence, independently validated, and used to inform decisions about tooling, infrastructure, and development practices

Software Development.text: How frequently is sustainability data from software development fed into the organisation's GHG inventory and sustainability reporting?
Software Development.option_a: No sustainability data from software development is captured or reported
Software Development.option_b: Data is provided to the Sustainability team on request, with no regular cadence
Software Development.option_c: An annual update is provided to the Sustainability team, based on available data
Software Development.option_d: Regular updates are provided — at least quarterly — with consistent methodology and coverage across the main development activities
Software Development.option_e: An automated data connection feeds sustainability-relevant data from development systems directly into the GHG inventory and sustainability reporting platform on a continuous basis

Software Development.text: Do your development teams benchmark the sustainability efficiency of their code and applications?
Software Development.option_a: Code is not benchmarked from a sustainability or efficiency perspective
Software Development.option_b: We trust that well-written code is inherently efficient but have no formal benchmarking process
Software Development.option_c: We have shared benchmarking studies or reference standards with teams, and some informal measurement occurs
Software Development.option_d: Formal benchmarking against sustainability KPIs is conducted for significant applications, with defined targets and results reviewed at team level
Software Development.option_e: Sustainability efficiency benchmarking is standard practice across the development portfolio, conducted regularly, independently validated in some cases, and tracked against targets that are reviewed and updated on a defined cycle

Software Development.text: Do software sustainability efficiency metrics feed back into product decisions, feature prioritisation, and business cases — or do they stop at team-level reporting?
Software Development.option_a: Software sustainability metrics, where they exist, are reported within engineering teams and do not influence product decisions, feature prioritisation, or budget conversations
Software Development.option_b: Sustainability data is occasionally referenced in product discussions, typically when a specific performance or cost issue surfaces a sustainability angle, but it does not systematically influence decisions
Software Development.option_c: Sustainability efficiency metrics are included in some product and service reviews; there are examples where inefficiency data has influenced a product decision, but the process is not systematic or consistent
Software Development.option_d: Software sustainability metrics are a formal input to product and service reviews; efficiency improvement targets are included in product roadmaps; significantly inefficient features or services can be deprioritised or redesigned on sustainability grounds
Software Development.option_e: Software efficiency metrics are fully integrated into the product decision framework: consumption intensity per feature, transaction, or user is tracked and used in prioritisation decisions; the business case process requires sustainability efficiency assessment for significant new development; product teams are accountable for the sustainability efficiency of what they ship, not just its functional performance; and avoided development — choosing not to build what cannot be justified on efficiency grounds — is a recognised outcome

Software Development.text: Do your development and product practices include active avoidance disciplines — challenging whether features, data, and services should exist at all, rather than only optimising how efficiently they run?
Software Development.option_a: Development practice focuses on building and optimising; there is no systematic practice of questioning whether existing features, data stores, or services should be retired or consolidated
Software Development.option_b: Technical debt reviews occasionally surface redundant services or data, but these are addressed for performance or cost reasons rather than as a sustainability avoidance discipline
Software Development.option_c: Some avoidance practices exist informally — for example, data retention policies, periodic feature usage reviews — but they are not framed as sustainability disciplines and are not consistently applied
Software Development.option_d: Formal avoidance disciplines are in place: data minimisation policies limit what is stored and for how long; feature usage is tracked and features with low adoption can be retired; service consolidation is a regular architectural exercise; and sustainability impact is an explicit rationale for each
Software Development.option_e: Demand avoidance is embedded in the development and product culture: every significant new feature or service requires justification that includes its sustainability cost; data minimisation is a default design principle; feature and service retirement is a regular governed process with sustainability outcomes tracked; and the organisation can demonstrate material reductions in compute, storage, and energy consumption attributable to avoidance decisions rather than efficiency optimisation

Software Development.text: Are developers trained to write efficient code and to understand the environmental impact of their development decisions?
Software Development.option_a: No training exists on efficient coding or the environmental impact of development decisions
Software Development.option_b: Developers use available tools and frameworks at their own discretion — no standards or training are in place
Software Development.option_c: Development communities or guilds include sustainability as a topic, and some guidance exists, but training is not mandatory or systematic
Software Development.option_d: Developers receive structured training on efficient coding practices and sustainability standards, with documented expectations and some tooling to support adherence
Software Development.option_e: Developers are comprehensively trained in sustainable coding practices, supported by automated tooling that enforces standards, flags inefficiencies, and reports on environmental impact as part of the standard development workflow

Software Development.text: Do developers engage with infrastructure and platform teams to understand the sustainability profile of the hardware and platforms their software runs on?
Software Development.option_a: No engagement occurs — software teams build and deploy without visibility of the infrastructure sustainability context
Software Development.option_b: Developers are aware of the hardware available to them for specific technical reasons but do not consider its sustainability profile
Software Development.option_c: Ad-hoc conversations occur between development and infrastructure teams on sustainability topics, typically during design phases of significant projects
Software Development.option_d: A defined process ensures development teams receive regular updates on the sustainability performance of the infrastructure and cloud platforms their software runs on, and this informs design decisions
Software Development.option_e: Sustainability data from infrastructure and platform teams is continuously available to developers in a usable format — integrated into development tooling or dashboards — and is actively used to inform architecture, deployment, and optimisation decisions

Software Development.text: How is sustainability performance in software development reported to leadership and connected to the wider GreenOps agenda?
Software Development.option_a: Sustainability performance in software development is not reported — there is nothing to report and no expectation to do so
Software Development.option_b: Occasional updates are shared informally with senior management, typically when a specific initiative has been completed
Software Development.option_c: An annual sustainability report covering development activities is produced and shared with the Sustainability function and senior IT leadership
Software Development.option_d: Regular reporting — at least quarterly — is produced covering development sustainability performance, shared across relevant functions including data centre, infrastructure, procurement, and finance, and used in governance discussions
Software Development.option_e: Development sustainability data is captured continuously and automatically integrated into the organisation's GreenOps reporting platform, available in real time to the Sustainability team, IT leadership, and relevant business stakeholders

End User Services.text: Who owns the Sustainability agenda for End User Services?
End User Services.option_d: Defined Sustainability role within IT for EUS
End User Services.option_e: Dedicated accountable function, integrated with corporate Sustainability strategy

End User Services.text: Do you have a budget for Sustainability initiatives in EUS?
End User Services.option_d: Defined but limited EUS Sustainability budget
End User Services.option_e: Dedicated recurring budget with measurable ROI in cost & emissions savings

End User Services.text: How are device refresh decisions triggered — are they driven by fixed time cycles, or by evidence of device condition, performance, and actual user need?
End User Services.option_a: Refresh is driven by fixed time cycles — typically three or four years — applied uniformly regardless of device condition, performance, or actual user need; the cycle is set by vendor support terms or IT policy and is not questioned
End User Services.option_b: Time-based cycles are the default but informal exceptions are made when devices are clearly still functional; there is no systematic process for assessing condition or extending life, and most devices are still refreshed on schedule
End User Services.option_c: Device condition and performance data is reviewed at refresh time and some devices are extended beyond the standard cycle where evidence supports it, but the process is inconsistent, time-based thinking still dominates, and lifecycle extension is not tracked as an outcome
End User Services.option_d: Refresh decisions are based on device condition, performance telemetry, and actual user need rather than fixed time cycles; devices remain in service until they cannot meet role requirements; the organisation tracks average device age and has demonstrably extended it compared to a fixed-cycle baseline
End User Services.option_e: Performance and condition-based refresh is the defined policy across all device categories; time-based cycles have been replaced with evidence-based assessment; devices are routinely extended, repaired, or redeployed to less demanding roles before retirement; and the organisation quantifies the carbon and cost saving from lifecycle extension versus what a fixed-cycle approach would have produced

End User Services.option_c: Option in some categories but not scaled
End User Services.option_d: Widely sourced where viable, supported by policy
End User Services.option_e: Refurb/renew default in procurement; new devices must be justified

End User Services.text: How are device selection standards set?
End User Services.option_d: Standards include embodied emissions, repairability, recyclability
End User Services.option_e: LCA/PCF, right-to-repair, modular design, and emissions thresholds are mandatory

End User Services.text: How do you measure environmental impact of devices?
End User Services.option_d: Device-level telemetry + embodied impact data included
End User Services.option_e: Full lifecycle (embodied + operational + circularity) reported per device, linked to corporate GHG accounting

End User Services.text: How are peripherals and accessories (monitors, headsets, keyboards, docks, adapters, cables) managed from a sustainability perspective?
End User Services.option_a: No sustainability consideration — peripherals and accessories are procured and disposed of without tracking
End User Services.option_b: Basic cost management only; some awareness of recycling options at end of life
End User Services.option_c: Sustainability criteria are applied to some peripheral categories (e.g. energy-efficient monitors), and basic end-of-life processes exist
End User Services.option_d: Sustainability standards are applied consistently across major peripheral and accessory categories; lifecycle management includes reuse and responsible disposal
End User Services.option_e: Full lifecycle management for peripherals and accessories: sustainability criteria in procurement, reuse and repair programmes, tracked end-of-life disposal, and embodied emissions included in EUS carbon reporting
Digital-first strategy: print minimised, emissions tracked and tied to KPIs

End User Services.text: How do you handle end-of-life for EUS equipment?
End User Services.option_d: ITAD partner provides detailed circularity/emissions metrics
End User Services.option_e: Full chain-of-custody with reuse/resale reporting, avoided emissions quantified

End User Services.text: How do you track energy consumption of devices?
End User Services.option_e: Real-time telemetry integrated into Sustainability dashboards and optimisation workflows

End User Services.text: How do you actively manage and reduce demand for EUS devices — through device sharing, pooling, or challenging refresh requests?
End User Services.option_a: Demand is not actively managed — refresh requests are fulfilled without challenge and device-per-user ratios are not reviewed
End User Services.option_b: Some awareness of demand management exists but there is no formal process or policy
End User Services.option_c: Refresh requests are reviewed against need in some cases; device pooling or sharing exists informally for some user types
End User Services.option_d: A formal demand management process exists: refresh requests require justification, device pooling is actively used for appropriate roles, and device-per-user ratios are tracked and reviewed
End User Services.option_e: Demand reduction is a primary EUS sustainability lever: device numbers are benchmarked against utilisation data, shared and pooled device programmes are embedded, refresh requests are systematically challenged, and avoided device procurement is quantified and reported as an environmental and cost outcome

End User Services.text: How are mobile devices (phones, tablets) managed?
End User Services.option_d: Lifecycle emissions and repairability factored into procurement
End User Services.option_e: Circular-first sourcing (renew, reuse, modular repair), emissions tracked

End User Services.text: Do you measure user behaviour impacts (energy, printing, peripheral use)?
End User Services.option_e: Continuous monitoring with behaviour nudges linked to Sustainability KPIs

End User Services.text: Do EUS Sustainability criteria influence actual procurement decisions?
End User Services.option_d: Regularly considered in procurement alongside cost/performance
End User Services.option_e: Sustainability is a decisive factor — suppliers/products rejected if they fail thresholds

End User Services.text: How is Sustainability data integrated into ITAM/GreenOps reporting?
End User Services.option_e: Fully integrated into ITAM/GreenOps tooling with automated device-level updates

End User Services.text: How aligned is EUS with regulatory standards (UK GBS, EPEAT, CSRD, EU taxonomy)?
End User Services.option_d: Formal alignment for major device categories
End User Services.option_e: Full regulatory compliance embedded; preparing for future rules with reporting and audits

End User Services.text: How do you manage the software configuration deployed on devices to optimise performance, reduce unnecessary bloat, and extend useful device life?
End User Services.option_a: Device software images are not actively managed for efficiency; standard vendor configurations are deployed with minimal modification and software accumulates over time without review or rationalisation
End User Services.option_b: Basic software management exists — for example, approved application lists — but image optimisation, bloat reduction, and performance management are not framed as sustainability considerations and do not influence refresh decisions
End User Services.option_c: Device images are reviewed and rationalised periodically; unnecessary pre-installed software is removed in some configurations; performance monitoring identifies poorly running devices, though root cause is not consistently investigated before refresh is triggered
End User Services.option_d: Software image management is a defined discipline: baseline images are optimised for performance and efficiency, unnecessary applications and background processes are removed as standard, and performance degradation triggers investigation before refresh is approved — software remediation is used to extend device life where hardware remains adequate
End User Services.option_e: Device software configuration is actively managed as a sustainability and lifecycle lever: optimised images are standard across all device categories, performance baselines are defined and monitored continuously, degradation triggers investigation rather than automatic refresh, and the organisation tracks the number of devices whose life has been extended through software intervention rather than hardware replacement; image management contributes measurably to fleet lifecycle targets and avoided procurement is reported as a sustainability outcome

End User Services.text: How do you manage the ratio of devices to users and challenge the assumption that every user requires a dedicated, full-specification device?
End User Services.option_a: Every user is allocated a dedicated device as a matter of policy; the assumption that each person needs their own full-specification device is not questioned; device-to-user ratios are not tracked
End User Services.option_b: Device-to-user ratios are broadly known but not actively managed; shared or pooled devices exist in a few areas — for example, shift workers or reception desks — but are not a systematic programme and do not reduce overall fleet size materially
End User Services.option_c: Device-to-user ratios are reviewed periodically and shared device programmes operate for some user segments; some effort is made to right-size specifications to actual workload requirements, but the process is not consistent and does not systematically challenge individual device allocation across the estate
End User Services.option_d: Fleet right-sizing is an active discipline: device-to-user ratios are benchmarked against role requirements; shared device, virtual desktop, and pooled programmes serve appropriate user segments at scale; device specifications are matched to actual workload needs; and the organisation can demonstrate measurable reductions in fleet size attributable to right-sizing activity
End User Services.option_e: The organisation challenges device allocation as a primary sustainability lever: every role type has a defined device entitlement based on genuine need; shared, pooled, and virtual device models serve all appropriate user segments; high-specification devices are justified and audited; device-to-user ratios are tracked as a KPI and are improving year on year; and avoided device procurement from right-sizing is quantified in both carbon and cost terms and reported alongside other EUS sustainability outcomes

End User Services.text: Does EUS sustainability and efficiency data feed actively into forward capacity planning and procurement decisions — reducing what is bought, not just improving what is already owned?
End User Services.option_a: EUS data is collected and reported but does not feed into forward procurement planning; volume decisions are driven by headcount and refresh cycles without reference to utilisation or sustainability evidence
End User Services.option_b: Utilisation and efficiency data is occasionally referenced in procurement planning, but it is used to inform specification choices rather than to challenge volumes; the number of devices procured is not systematically reduced as a result
End User Services.option_c: EUS efficiency data influences some procurement decisions — for example, lower utilisation evidence has deferred some refresh activity — but there is no formal feedback loop from sustainability data to procurement planning and avoidance is not a stated objective
End User Services.option_d: A formal feedback loop exists between EUS efficiency and utilisation data and procurement planning: volume forecasts are adjusted based on evidence, low-utilisation device pools are identified and reduce future procurement need, and sustainability efficiency data is a defined input to the annual procurement plan
End User Services.option_e: EUS sustainability data drives procurement avoidance as a first-order discipline: device utilisation, efficiency, and lifecycle data feed continuously into procurement planning and demonstrably reduce volumes procured; avoided procurement is tracked and reported as a sustainability and cost outcome; the procurement model is structured to reward avoidance — buying fewer, longer-lived, higher-quality devices — over replacing like-for-like on schedule
